author: @Nooby00815
text:
Excellent and most interesting video. I could not resist, I had to print it for my own. Unfortunately my first try was not completely successful. The gear it self works great, but unfortunately the inner shaft slips through the inner ring of the gear, so I'm not able to rotate it on the handle, I have to rotate the inner ring of the gear directly. As soon as I find the time, I'll rework the shaft and the inner ring to a rectangular connection (like on the handle). I think then it will work great. :)
Once again thank you so much, it's so much fun to work with it.

author: @RJTC
text:
Neat idea, however the angle position of the parallel-sided rollers will cause slip and wear - they would need to be tapered and the "race" angles adjusted, something like in a taper-roller bearing, to run without skidding. 
[The outer diameter of the taper race has larger circumference than the inner, so a parallel roller cannot run in a circular path without slip or skid].

author: @dansw0rkshop
text: This is essentially a planetary gear reduction. Automatic transmissions use sets of these cascaded together with an assemblage of band clutches, clutch packs, and sprag clutches to do their thing.  Your 3d-printed gear is interesting, but nothing new.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yes it is - but the same sense you can say a bearing is a sort of planetary gear and on that argument the NIF drive wasn't new either, neither is the Archimedes drive, nor  the evan differential - it's a bit of a spurious argument to say it is essentially the same as something else - lots of things have stuff in common and lots things have differences in spite of their commonality - after all a gear is just a wheel with teeth on it so nothing is new since the pointy stick - do you see what i mean here mate - this is a bit reducto in absurdum for sure - but do you see what I am saying? Just because it is essentially similar doesn't mean it isn't different too

author: @marcfruchtman9473
text: This might have substantial utility for Moving Machinery along the Ways, where you have to move say a table or tool along a path with precision.  There are many different methods to do this such as Lead screw and nut mechanism, rack and pinion, linear motors, and belt drive. But, all of these have issue with Backlash to one degree or another. However, ball screws tend to have a fairly low amount of Backlash, but they are fairly expensive to machine... this could be a low cost break thru replacement for the ball screw.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I like that mate - i was pondering the load bearing capabilities of this and how it would behave both radially and axially

replies.author: @marcfruchtman9473
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering I can't be certain without playing around with it. It does remind me of thrust roller bearings. I would say that The softer the cylinder, the more friction there will be, and thus more mobility. You do get improved traction with TPU, but you will get more precision with harder cylinders. 
I would say this - You might consider swapping out the projections with a projection that inserts into a bearing and make sure that the cylinder ends don't ride along that bottom taper and top taper, since that just adds to the friction and doesn't really improve the drive as far as I can tell. (Over time, it will chew into your taper too). Of course you still want the sides of the cylinder to ride along the taper.  I am unsure about the axial right now... have to see how it behaves. But if it is like other Thrust Roller Bearings, it should be fairly good. To improve the "radial" load, you could use a pair of these opposing each other.

author: @ceptimus
text: Did you make the TPU parts slightly cone-shaped, to account for them needing to roll further on the outer edge of their 'tracks'?  Without that enhancement, they'll need to skid a bit as they go around, but if you did make them cone-shaped, you have to be careful to assemble them the correct way round.

author: @timmyssweet
text:
It's an interesting idea.  For simplicity, I think it might be better to put the cylinder perpendicular to two rings.  I would also consider covering skater bearings with TPU.  This would allow less friction and more pressure on the TPU to avoid slippage, but the greaseless drive is worth investigating.

Well done.  

I've been boggled as to how to move solar panels cost effectively, aesthetically, and safe from storms.  I like the idea of lots of small solar panels moving with the sun, but trackers are expensive and so too are small panels compared to large ones.  Perhaps, a turn table like that might work.

author: @mfree80286
text:
Friction planetary drive, seems very close to a CVT drive Sterling was attempting in the late 80's that used tilting discs rolling against toroids. That, actually, would be quite interesting to see an attempt at a 3D printed module. 

For "New" new, still not truly new, I've had an idea swirling in my head for some time about adapting a recirculating ball mechanism with a movable guide race that changes the angle the ball train interacts with the pinion for an effective gear ratio change. Would need some manner of accumulator on the slack side since the 'chain' differs in length over operation.

author: @user-hf3ym7lh4d
text:
Always enjoy your videos, thank you!

A small correction: "backdriving" doesn't refer to rotating in both directions. It means you can apply torque to either side to turn the other side, e.g. using the input as the output and vice-versa.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Thanks for that - you are right - of course- but it can still be back driven and i should have shown that

author: @GeoffTV2
text: Great video and demo of concept as always, thank you for sharing. Referencing the thrust race demo at about 3:45, some electric model race cars utilise this kind of thing for their differentials. Instead of having a geared differential, they have a ball differential. The setup is preloaded to limit slip. A quick search for "ball differential" will get a diagram for anyone who wants to see. Companies like Tamiya make them.
